ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Raita. Nearest parent is a yogurt-based condiment with vegetables. A new concept for Indian raita preparations is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Yogurt and cucumber condiment, spiced (closest parent)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of raita is 1 small bowl (approximately 100g) — a cool, smooth, white yogurt condiment with diced cucumber, a dusting of roasted cumin and chilli, and fresh coriander, served cold alongside biryani or paratha.

nutrient_source.title: Raita — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F01040003; cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match in HPB Foodo under food code F01040003 — Raita (cucumber and yogurt, spiced), standard preparation.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from plain yogurt with cucumber; the yogurt protein and fat give a very low GI; the cucumber adds negligible carbohydrates.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 225mg per 100g serving — moderate; the chaat masala and salt are the sodium sources. The calcium from yogurt (102mg/100g) is beneficial for blood pressure.
hypertension.guidance: Raita is an excellent accompaniment for hypertensive patients — the calcium from yogurt is beneficial, and it adds a low-calorie, low-fat cooling element to a meal. Reduce chaat masala to lower sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI (~35); glycaemic load per 100g serving is approximately 2 — negligible. Excellent choice as a low-calorie, low-GI accompaniment that can moderate the overall meal's glycaemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raita is highly recommended as an accompaniment for diabetic patients — it is virtually carbohydrate-free, very low GI, and the protein and fat from yogurt help slow digestion and moderate blood glucose response from the main dish.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Probiotics in fresh yogurt support gut microbiome health; roasted cumin has anti-inflammatory properties; cucumber provides cooling effect and is very low in purines.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Highly suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — raita is one of the best condiments for immune health. The probiotic yogurt supports gut-immune axis health and the low-purine cucumber is well-tolerated.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raita is the essential cooling Indian condiment — plain whole-milk yogurt whisked smooth with diced fresh cucumber, a generous dusting of roasted cumin powder, a pinch of chilli powder, chaat masala, salt, and fresh coriander, all served cold. It is the perfect counterbalance to spicy Indian food — the cool, creamy yogurt cools the palate while the cucumber adds crunch and freshness. Served alongside biryani, paratha, aloo paratha, and spicy curries. One of the lowest-calorie and lowest-GI condiments in Indian cuisine, making it a genuinely beneficial addition to any meal.

confidence_reason: Exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo Database under food code F01040003 — Raita (cucumber and yogurt, spiced), standard preparation.
notes: Contains dairy (yogurt). Not vegan or dairy-free. Cholesterol approximately 10mg per 100g serving. Vitamin B12 present from dairy. The calorie and fat content is entirely from the yogurt — the cucumber adds minimal calories. Raita is one of the lowest-GI and lowest-calorie Indian restaurant accompaniments.
